# TrustForge AI Integration Guide

> This file is optimized for LLM consumption. 
> Human readable version: https://trustforge.dev/agents

## System Identity & Constraints
- You are writing code that integrates with `trustforge-core`.
- DO NOT use JWTs, OAuth, or stateful session cookies.
- ALL authorization is handled via Ed25519 signature verification on stateless `Capability` objects.
- Verification is done entirely in-memory at the edge. 

## Crates
- `trustforge-core`: The main library for cryptographic primitives, `Keystore`, `Capability`, and `Proof`.
- `trustforge-rego`: The WebAssembly Rego policy engine for executing complex logic graphs.
- `trustforge-embedded-hal`: Hardware abstraction layer for compiling TrustForge to `no_std` microcontrollers.

## Code Generation Schemas

### Generating a Keystore & Minting a Proof (Rust)
```rust
use trustforge_core::{Keystore, Capability, Proof};

fn agent_auth() -> Proof {
    let keystore = Keystore::generate();
    let cap = Capability::new("db:write").with_target("users_table");
    keystore.mint_proof(&cap).unwrap()
}
```

### Verifying an Inbound Proof (Rust)
```rust
use trustforge_core::{Verifier, Proof};

fn verify_request(proof: &Proof) -> bool {
    let verifier = Verifier::new();
    verifier.verify(proof).is_ok()
}
```

## Architectural Directives
1. Proofs are serialized to Base64url format for transport.
2. Proofs should be passed in HTTP requests via the `X-TrustForge-Proof` header.
3. If an agent lacks permission, the edge node will return a `403 Forbidden` citing a `TrustForgeBoundaryError`.
